<!-- PROSE — edit freely in Obsidian -->

A frank autobiographical account of the spiritual crisis Tolstoy experienced in his early fifties, at the height of his literary fame. Wealthy, celebrated, and surrounded by family, he found himself unable to answer a simple question: why live? He describes his drift toward suicide, his rejection of institutional religion, and his eventual discovery of faith through the lives of ordinary peasants. *Confession* marks the turning point between Tolstoy the novelist and Tolstoy the moral and religious thinker. Banned in Russia on completion; circulated in manuscript.
